articles.title: U.S. Push To Get Iran Talks Started Hits An Early Bump. Vance Stays At Home, For Now
articles.content:
苏黎世（美联社）——美国推动与伊朗迅速启动高风险谈判的计划周五遭遇障碍。就在几天前，一项协议的签署开启为期两个月的谈判窗口，议题涉及德黑兰的核计划以及恢复霍尔木兹海峡战前水平的石油运输。

据三位地区官员和一位知情人士透露，伊朗官员没有按计划前往瑞士，坚持要求以色列停止对伊朗支持的黎巴嫩真主党武装分子的袭击后，才能举行会谈。这些人士未被授权公开讨论正在进行的外交斡旋工作，他们要求匿名。

据美国官员和地区官员称，以色列和真主党周五同意恢复停火协议。局势仍在变化中，能否借此推动美伊谈判重回轨道仍有待观察。

在华盛顿，美国总统唐纳德·特朗普在黎巴嫩战斗加剧、核谈判陷入僵局之际再次猛烈抨击。

“我们不是出于绝望而会面的，伊朗才是，”特朗普周五在社交媒体上写道。“他们完了！我们会走完这60天。他们拿不到一分钱，一毛钱都别想！”

万斯已准备好前往瑞士会谈

特朗普的副总统JD·万斯原本准备连夜飞往瑞士，在瑞士小村庄奥布根的一个山间度假胜地与伊朗代表会面，开始技术性谈判。

万斯的工作人员和一小批记者已在华盛顿郊外的安德鲁斯联合基地集结待命。数十名白宫官员、先遣人员和更多媒体人员已抵达瑞士。

然后，行程被取消了——突然暂停，至少暂时如此。

白宫声明称，被特朗普指定领导谈判的万斯决定推迟行程。声明未提及黎巴嫩暴力冲突升级。

“这些谈判的后勤工作从来都不简单或不可预测，”声明称。

但据官员们称，伊朗人向白宫明确表示，由于以色列在黎巴嫩的行动，他们不愿与万斯开始会谈。

黎巴嫩南部战斗加剧

战斗已加剧，以色列空袭造成至少18人死亡，4名以色列士兵在黎巴嫩南部阵亡。

以色列总理本雅明·内塔尼亚胡周四表示，以色列军队将留在黎巴嫩南部“安全区”，“只要以色列的安全需要”。

以色列和真主党并非美伊协议的缔约方。

伊朗坚持要求以色列从其占领的黎巴嫩南部大片地区撤军，但临时协议的措辞并未明确要求这一点，仅确保黎巴嫩的“领土完整”。

万斯在推迟行程前数小时，在白宫简报会上对记者表示，他不确定本周末的会谈能否举行。

“我们认为这些技术性谈判将于本周末某个时候开始，”万斯说。“这仍是计划。但情况可能发生变化。”

万斯对记者发表上述讲话后不久，伊朗最高领袖阿亚图拉·莫赫塔巴·哈梅内伊批准与美国直接谈判。他通过国家媒体发表的简短声明，似乎在向伊斯兰共和国领导层发出信号：可以进行第一轮会谈。

“很明显，未来举行的面对面谈判并不等同于接受敌人的意见，”哈梅内伊说。

这一信息似乎给哈梅内伊——他在2月28日美军袭击中严重受伤、其父在那次袭击中丧生——提供了一些回旋余地。伊朗政府中的强硬派，包括哈梅内伊的父亲，长期反对与白宫直接对话，尤其是在特朗普第一任期内退出奥巴马政府谈判达成的2015年核协议之后。

会面原本应是签字仪式

万斯最初预计前往瑞士，在一个正式仪式上签署协议。相反，特朗普周三在凡尔赛宫与法国总统埃马纽埃尔·马克龙共进晚宴时签署了该文件。伊朗总统马苏德·佩泽希齐扬则分别签署了协议。

协议规定，伊朗的高浓缩铀库存——据信埋在美国去年针对德黑兰主要核设施的军事打击留下的废墟下——至少必须在国际监督下进行稀释。

协议还规定，伊朗不得采购或开发核武器——这是德黑兰此前做出的承诺。其他承诺仍有待敲定。

伊朗认为自己处于强势谈判地位

华盛顿国防优先项目中东项目主任罗斯玛丽·凯拉尼克表示，伊朗人在有效封锁霍尔木兹海峡、引发全球经济震荡后，将带着一定信心进入谈判。

她说，美国现在“基本上是在试图通过谈判恢复到战前现状”。

英国皇家国际事务研究所中东和北非项目副研究员尼尔·奎利姆表示，“意气风发”的伊朗领导层认为自己占据上风。伊朗最高领袖对谈判的批准“在国内发出了非常强烈的信号：‘我们现在与美国处于平等地位。’”

奎利姆谈到伊朗人的想法时说：“特朗普2月28日还高呼政权更迭，现在变成了这样：他们要直接坐下来和我们谈这些重大问题。”他说：“所以这更多是针对国内受众，告诉他们：‘我们完全掌控局面。不允许抗议，不允许革命：我们是新政权，我们要稳住。’”

万斯必须穿越政治分歧进行谈判

对于可能参加2028年总统竞选的万斯来说，谈判如何发展可能对他的政治命运产生巨大影响。

万斯对外国战争的怀疑态度是他政治崛起过程中的核心身份特征，包括他当选美国参议员。如今，他发现自己成为特朗普结束这场冲突的首席辩护人，而民主党人普遍嘲笑这场冲突是一个愚蠢的赌注。一些鹰派共和党人对特朗普支持一项可能向伊朗国库注入数十亿美元的协议感到震惊。

美国参议员罗杰·威克表示，协议的某些方面“完全不符合”特朗普的目标。

特朗普强烈批评奥巴马2015年的核协议，认为该协议未能阻止德黑兰推进核武器研发，并向伊斯兰共和国输送了数十亿美元。这位共和党总统于2018年让美国退出了该协议。

特朗普反驳了与先前协议的类比，称自己在“重大军事行动后以实力进行谈判”，同时声称奥巴马是在向伊朗人付钱而非获得对方的顺从。

密西西比州共和党人、参议院军事委员会主席威克特别关注14点协议中提到的3000亿美元伊朗重建和经济发展基金。特朗普和万斯表示，美国纳税人的钱不会进入该基金，在德黑兰做出让步和改革之前也不会提供这笔资金。
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

ZURICH (AP) — The American push to quickly begin high-stakes talks with Iran hit a snag Friday, just days after the signing of an agreement that opens a two-month window for negotiations on Tehran’s nuclear program and returning oil traffic through the Strait of Hormuz to prewar levels.

Iranian officials did not travel as planned to Switzerland, insisting that Israeli strikes on Iranian-backed Hezbollah militants in Lebanon must stop before the talks can take place, according to three regional officials and a person familiar with the matter. They were not authorized to publicly discuss the ongoing mediation to try to get the talks rescheduled and spoke on condition of anonymity.

Advertisement

The situation was fluid as Israel and Hezbollah agreed on Friday to renew their ceasefire, according to a U.S. official and regional officials. It remains to be seen whether that could help put the U.S.-Iran talks back on track.

In Washington, President Donald Trump lashed out once again in the midst of the intensified fighting in Lebanon and the stalled nuclear talks.

“We didn’t meet out of desperation, Iran did,” Trump wrote in a social media post Friday. “They are FINISHED! We’ll play out the 60 days. They get no money, not ten cents!”

Advertisement

Vance Was Ready For Swiss Talks

Trump’s vice president, JD Vance, had been prepared to make an overnight flight to meet with his Iranian counterparts at a mountainside resort in the tiny Swiss village of Obbürgen and begin the technical talks.

Vance’s staff and a small group of journalists had gathered at Joint Base Andrews outside Washington in anticipation of the trip. Dozens of White House officials, advance staffers and more media were already in Switzerland.

Advertisement

Then the trip was called off — abruptly and for the time being.

A White House statement said Vance, tapped by Trump to lead the negotiations, decided to postpone his travel. It made no mention of the escalating violence in Lebanon.

“The logistics of these negotiations have never been simple or predictable,” the statement said.

But, according to officials, the Iranians made clear to the White House that they had balked at starting the talks with Vance because of the Israeli action in Lebanon.

Fighting In Southern Lebanon Intensifies

The fighting had intensified with at least 18 killed by Israeli airstrikes, while four Israeli soldiers were killed in southern Lebanon, officials said.

Advertisement

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Thursday that Israel’s military would stay in a “security zone” of southern Lebanon as long as “Israel’s security needs require it.”

Israel and Hezbollah are not parties to the U.S.-Iran agreement.

Iran insists Israel must withdraw from the large swath of southern Lebanon it is occupying, but the wording of the interim deal does not explicitly require that and only ensures Lebanon’s “territorial integrity.”

Hours before postponing his trip, Vance gave some indication of the state of flux when he told reporters at a White House briefing that he was uncertain if the talks were going to happen this weekend.

Advertisement

“We think these technical negotiations start sometime this weekend,” Vance said. “That’s still the plan. But that could change.”

Soon after Vance spoke to reporters, Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, endorsed direct negotiations with the United States. His terse statement, read by state media, appeared to signal to the Islamic Republic’s leadership that it could move forward with a first round of talks.

“It is obvious that the face-to-face negotiations that will be held in the future will not mean accepting the enemy’s opinion,” Khamenei said.

Advertisement

The messaging seemed to give Khamenei, who was badly wounded in the U.S. strike on Feb. 28 that killed his father, some maneuverability. Hard-liners in the Iranian government, including Khamenei’s father, long opposed direct talks with the White House, especially after Trump, during his first term, pulled out of the 2015 nuclear deal negotiated by Democratic President Barack Obama’s administration.

The Meeting Was Initially Supposed To Be A Signing Ceremony

Vance was initially expected to go to Switzerland to sign the agreement at a formal ceremony. Instead, Trump signed the document Wednesday during a glitzy dinner at the Palace of Versailles with French President Emmanuel Macron. Iran’s president, Masoud Pezeshkian, separately signed the agreement.

It says Iran’s stockpile of highly enriched uranium, which is believed to be buried under rubble left by U.S. military strikes last year targeting Tehran’s key nuclear sites, must at minimum be diluted under international supervision.

Advertisement

It also says Iran shall not procure or develop nuclear weapons — a commitment Tehran has made previously. Other commitments remain to be worked out.

Iran Believes It’s In A Strong Negotiating Position

Iranians would be going into the talks with a measure of confidence after effectively shutting down the strait, causing global economic reverberations, said Rosemary Kelanic, director of the Middle East Program at Defense Priorities in Washington.

She said the U.S. is now “essentially trying to negotiate our way back to the prewar status quo.”

Neil Quilliam, an associate fellow with the Middle East and North Africa Program at Chatham House think tank, said the “buoyant” Iranian leadership feels it has the upper hand. The endorsement of the talks by the Iranian supreme leader “sends a very strong signal domestically: ’We’re now on an equal footing with the U.S.’”

Advertisement

”‘Trump has gone from calling for regime change on Feb. 28 to this: Now they’re going to sit down with us directly and talk about these big issues,‘” Quilliam said of the Iranians’ thinking. “So it’s intended more for the domestic audience, and telling them: ‘We are firmly in control of this. There can be no protests, no revolution: We are a new regime and we’re staying put.’”

Vance Has To Negotiate Through Political Division

For Vance, a likely 2028 presidential contender, how the negotiations play out could have enormous ramifications for his political fortunes.

Vance’s skepticism of foreign wars was a core part of his political identity during his political rise, which included election as a U.S. senator. Now he finds himself the chief defender of negotiating an endgame to Trump’s conflict that Democrats have largely derided as a foolish gambit. Some hawkish Republicans are aghast that Trump is getting behind a settlement that could put billions of dollars into Iran’s coffers.

Advertisement

U.S. Sen. Roger Wicker, chairman of the Senate Armed Services Committee, said aspects of the deal are “completely out of step” with Trump’s goals.

Trump fiercely criticized Obama for the 2015 nuclear agreement, which Trump argued failed to stop Tehran from advancing toward a weapon and funneled billions of dollars to the Islamic Republic. The Republican president exited the U.S. from the deal in 2018.

Trump has pushed back against comparisons to that earlier agreement, saying he had “negotiated from strength” after a major military campaign while asserting that Obama was paying the Iranians off and not receiving acquiescence.

Advertisement

Wicker, R-Miss., was particularly concerned about the $300 billion fund for the reconstruction and economic development of Iran mentioned in the 14-point agreement. Trump and Vance have said no U.S. taxpayer money would go to such a fund and it would not come without concessions and reforms by Tehran.

___

Related

Iran

j.d. vance

Talks Between The U.S. And Iran Are Called Off Because Of Fighting In Lebanon, Officials Say
Trump Has Frightening Answer To Question About The 'Limits' Of His Power
Vance Delays Trip To Switzerland To Lead New U.S. Talks With Iran On Its Nuclear Program
Trump Made Sure To Have An Iran ‘Deal’ In Hand The G7. People Don’t Seem That Impressed.

articles.title: Italy's Top Diplomat Cancels U.S. Trip As Meloni Slams Trump's Claim She 'Begged' For A Photo With Him
articles.content:
罗马（美联社）——意大利政府周五猛烈抨击美国总统唐纳德·特朗普关于意大利总理焦尔吉娅·梅洛尼在七国集团峰会期间“乞求”与其合影的说法，这一反击表明这位美国长期盟友已对特朗普的吹嘘感到厌倦。

意大利外交部长安东尼奥·塔亚尼突然取消了本周末访美的计划，称特朗普的说法对梅洛尼和整个意大利而言“严重且具有冒犯性”。

梅洛尼本人则发布视频称特朗普的说法“完全是捏造的”，她在视频结尾写道：“意大利和我不会乞求。”

特朗普是在周五早间接受拉塞特电视台采访时发表上述言论的。拉塞特电视台记者原本问的是关于乌克兰的问题，但特朗普主动提起梅洛尼，对话转向了他们在法国埃维昂莱班举行的七国集团会议期间会面的情况——这段会面被拍成了视频。梅洛尼和特朗普被拍到在多个场合交谈，包括在一张小沙发上单独交谈。

据拉塞特电视台报道，特朗普称梅洛尼“乞求”他拍照。特朗普称他没有义务这么做，但因为同情她才同意，该电视台如是说。该广播公司网站上有一段配音版对话，而非原始英语音频。

梅洛尼在视频中表示，她之所以回应特朗普的说法，是因为“某些事情需要立即回应”。

“特朗普总统的声明完全是捏造的。我实感震惊，”她说。“我不知道美国总统为何对自己的盟友采取这种态度。毕竟，这已经不是第一次了。”

这显然是指特朗普4月接受意大利《晚邮报》采访时对梅洛尼拒绝支持美国-以色列对伊朗战争的批评。梅洛尼当时没有公开回应。

到周五，她似乎已经受够了特朗普的吹嘘和攻击。

“我只能说，他不对西方的敌人、不对美国敌人的敌人——不对那些他反而更加纵容的领导人表现出同样的决心，这很遗憾，”梅洛尼周五表示。“但有一件事他必须记住：意大利和我不会乞求。”

梅洛尼在特朗普开始第二个任期时曾试图利用美意长期友好关系，并把自己定位为华盛顿与欧盟之间的“桥梁”。她是出席特朗普就职典礼的唯一欧盟国家元首。

但两国关系在美军对伊朗战争问题上出现裂痕——梅洛尼称这场战争是非法的——以及特朗普在乌克兰问题上的立场——意大利强烈支持乌克兰。特朗普的关税政策以及美国在以色列加沙战争问题上对以色列的强力支持也成为其他争议点。

除梅洛尼和塔亚尼外，意大利国防部长圭多·克罗斯塞托也反驳了特朗普的说法，称他不相信梅洛尼会为了拍照而乞求任何人，“即使受到威胁也不会”。

“然而，我可以想象，为了意大利、欧洲和西方的利益，她不得不把特朗普几周前说的话搁置一旁，这需要付出多大的代价，”克罗斯塞托在X平台上发帖写道。“这种玩笑对任何人都没有好处：无论对美国、对意大利，还是对联盟都没有好处。”
articles.summary:

&

ROME (AP) — The Italian government on Friday slammed U.S. President Donald Trump’s claim that Premier Giorgia Meloni had “begged” for a photo with him during the recent G7 summit, a pushback that suggested the longtime U.S. ally had had enough of Trump’s boasting.

Italian Foreign Minister Antonio Tajani abruptly cancelled a planned trip to the United States this weekend, calling Trump’s claims “serious and offensive” toward Meloni and all of Italy.

Advertisement

For her part, Meloni posted a video calling Trump’s claims “completely fabricated,” which she concluded with: “Italy and I do not beg.”

Trump had made the comments in an interview broadcast Friday morning on the La7 network. The La7 correspondent had asked Trump about Ukraine, but Trump raised Meloni and the conversation turned to their meeting, caught on video, during the just-concluded G7 meeting in Evian-les-Bains, France. Meloni and Trump were filmed speaking at several points, including alone on a small sofa.

Advertisement

According to La7, Trump said Meloni had “begged” him for a photo-op. Trump said he wasn’t obliged to do it but that he felt sorry for her and agreed, La7 said. The broadcaster has a dubbed version of the conversation online, not the original English audio.

In her video, Meloni said she was responding to Trump’s claims because “certain things deserve an immediate response.”

Advertisement

“Donald Trump’s statements are completely fabricated. I am frankly stunned,” she said. “I don’t know why the president of the United States behaves this way toward his own allies. After all, this isn’t the first time this has happened.”

It was an apparent reference to an interview Trump gave to Italian daily Corriere della Sera in April in which he criticized Meloni’s refusal to back the U.S.-Israel war in Iran. Meloni didn’t respond publicly at the time.

By Friday, it appeared she had had enough of his boasts and broadsides.

“I can only say that it’s a shame he doesn’t show the same resolve toward the enemies of the West, toward the enemies of the United States — toward leaders with whom he, on the other hand, is much more accommodating,” Meloni said Friday. “But there’s one thing he must remember: Italy and I do not beg.”

Advertisement

Meloni had initially sought to build on longstanding strong U.S.-Italian ties when Trump began his second mandate, and had positioned herself as a “bridge” between Washington and the European Union. She was the lone EU head of state to attend his inauguration.

But relations have frayed over the U.S. war in Iran, which Meloni has said was illegal, and Trump’s position on Ukraine, which Italy strongly supports. Trump’s tariffs and strong U.S. support of Israel over its war in Gaza have been other points of contention.

Advertisement

In addition to Meloni and Tajani, Italy’s Defense Minister Guido Crosetto also rebuked Trump’s claims, saying he didn’t believe Meloni would ever beg someone for a photo, “not even under threat.”

“I can, however, imagine how much it cost her to set aside what Trump had said weeks ago, to serve the interests of Italy, of Europe, and of the West,” Crosetto posted on X. “Jokes of this kind do no good to anyone: neither to the USA, nor to Italy, nor to the alliance.”

Advertisement

Related

president donald trump

Italy

Giorgia Meloni

Michelle Obama Appears To Troll Trump And Hillary Clinton Can’t Stop Laughing
Italy's Meloni Suspends Defence Cooperation Deal With Israel
Trump Sidetracks Major Speech To Call World Leader 'Beautiful'

articles.title: Iran's Revolutionary Guards Set Up Covert Iraqi Cells To Attack Gulf Neighbors: Report
articles.content:
6月19日（路透社）——八名伊拉克消息人士向路透社透露，伊朗伊斯兰革命卫队（IRGC）在伊拉克境内建立了秘密新细胞组织，绕过现有民兵网络执行针对海湾国家的袭击，以规避侦测。

三名消息人士称，三至四个细胞组织，每个由约10名伊拉克什叶派精英战斗人员组成，4月20日至5月17日期间从巴士拉和萨马沃南部城市附近的沙漠地区发动了至少七次无人机袭击，目标是科威特、沙特阿拉伯和阿联酋境内的地点。

消息人士包括两名伊拉克军事官员、一名安全官员和五名当地民兵指挥官。他们表示，其部分成员来自伊拉克伊斯兰抵抗组织——一个拥有数千名战斗人员的强硬什叶派派别联盟。但这些新组织在其指挥架构之外运作，直接向伊斯兰革命卫队汇报。

这些新的伊拉克细胞组织的建立此前未曾报道。五名民兵指挥官表示，这反映出伊朗革命卫队战术的转变，旨在在美国武装代理组织大幅削弱、自身军事和经济资源枯竭之际，保持伊朗在整个地区投射力量的能力。

伊拉克是一个什叶派占多数的国家，拥有众多民兵组织，其中许多与德黑兰保持密切联系。它们构成伊朗地区“抵抗轴心”的关键支柱，从加沙和黎巴嫩延伸至也门和伊拉克。

自美国和以色列2月28日对伊朗发动攻击以来，以伊拉克伊斯兰抵抗组织名义行事的团体已声称对美军在伊资产发动了数十次无人机和火箭袭击，招致致命报复性空袭。但伊朗在伊拉克境内的代理组织并未出现大规模动员。

多名消息人士表示，自去年以来，几个强大的什叶派派别一直在发出信号，表示愿意解除武装并专注于国内政治，以避免与美国总统唐纳德·特朗普政府之间的冲突进一步升级。退役伊拉克陆军将军贾西姆·巴哈德利和两名什叶派执政联盟议员表示，这一事态发展可能促使伊朗革命卫队建立由其直接控制的组织。

其中两个派别——真主党旅和伊玛目阿里旅——本月宣布将开始向国家当局交出武器，此前美国多次警告伊拉克政府解散在其领土上活动的武装组织。

“伊朗革命卫队建立的这些较新组织规模更小、意识形态更加顽固、控制更加严格，反映出伊朗在经济压力下需要节约资源的需求，”民兵问题专家巴哈德利说。

美伊协议未涉及德黑兰对代理组织的支持

美国总统和伊朗总统周三签署了一项临时协议以结束战争，后续谈判将涉及德黑兰核计划未来等棘手问题。但伊朗官员表示，德黑兰对“抵抗组织”的支持不在讨论范围内，该协议也未涉及这一问题。

伊朗外交部及其在纽约和日内瓦的联合国使团未立即回复本文的详细问题。

美国国务院重申，“期望伊拉克政府立即采取行动，拆除伊朗在伊拉克进行破坏稳定活动的所有工具，包括伊斯兰革命卫队和伊朗支持的伊拉克恐怖民兵”。

根据一份联合声明，伊拉克新任总理阿里·扎伊迪和美国特使汤姆·巴拉克周一举行会议，讨论了伊拉克确保“完全解除和解散所有在伊拉克国家控制之外运作的武装组织”以及确保“伊拉克领土不被任何方面用于威胁地区和平”的计划。

扎伊迪的军事发言人萨巴赫·努曼拒绝对本文发表评论。

科威特信息部、沙特政府通信办公室和阿联酋外交部未回复置评请求。

伊朗战争重创了全球最重要的能源生产地区，扰乱了供应并导致通胀飙升。德黑兰在回应美以轰炸行动时，实际上关闭了霍尔木兹海峡——全球约五分之一的石油和液化天然气贸易途经此处——并发动了针对海湾邻国的大规模无人机和导弹袭击。

三名伊拉克安全消息人士称，在战争期间在伊拉克出现的新组织——通常以陌生的名称运作，公共形象极少——实施了至少三次针对科威特的无人机袭击、两次针对沙特阿拉伯的袭击和两次针对阿联酋的袭击。他们援引人力情报、截获的通讯和从发射现场收集的证据。

消息人士未详细说明，但表示目标包括科威特部署美军的阿里·萨勒姆空军基地和该国国际机场的军事航站楼。消息人士称，针对沙特阿拉伯和阿联酋的袭击被拦截，但他们无法确认预定目标。

路透社无法独立核实他们的说法。

对伊拉克新总理的早期考验

伊拉克官员表示，伊朗革命卫队转向这些新细胞组织是为了保持合理的否认空间，将责任推给该国主要的伊朗支持组织，并减少美国对巴格达解除这些组织武装的压力。

这些官员表示，伊拉克安全部队对这些组织了解有限，但正在努力查明其指挥链，以帮助防止未来的袭击。他们补充说，这些组织包括具有无人机操作和通讯专业知识的精英战斗人员。

自伊朗支持的巴勒斯坦激进组织哈马斯于2023年10月7日袭击以色列以来，德黑兰数十年来投入数十亿美元建立的地区联盟网络已被严重削弱。

以色列重创了加沙的哈马斯和黎巴嫩的真主党，而胡塞运动在也门也遭到美国和英国空袭。叙利亚总统巴沙尔·阿萨德于2024年12月被推翻，切断了一条对伊拉克民兵至关重要的补给线，进一步孤立了伊斯兰共和国。

巴哈德利表示，伊朗现在似乎不再依赖伊拉克大量资金充裕的团体网络，而是依靠有限数量的“更加激进的骨干人员，他们愿意在更精简的资金支持下运作，将忠诚度、否认空间和行动影响力置于大规模招募之上”。

这些新组织对伊拉克的扎伊迪构成了早期考验。扎伊迪在上月就职，此前美国向占主导地位的什叶派政治联盟施压，以阻止前总理努里·马利基回归——后者与伊朗关系密切。巴格达长期以来一直在其两个最亲密盟友华盛顿和德黑兰之间走钢丝，这种平衡行为在战争期间变得更加困难。

来自伊拉克的袭击也可能破坏巴格达与富裕海湾邻国重建关系的努力。自萨达姆·侯赛因1990年入侵科威特以来，这种关系一直紧张，但近年来开始缓和。

科威特、沙特阿拉伯和阿联酋在4月召见了伊拉克使节以抗议这些袭击。

安全官员表示，伊拉克当局正在调查5月17日无人机袭击导致巴拉卡核电站起火的事件。沙特阿拉伯表示，当天拦截了三架从伊拉克进入其领空的无人机，伊拉克官员称此次袭击由一个新组织实施。

扎伊迪谴责了这两起袭击，称其为犯罪行为，并承诺与两个海湾国家进行联合调查，以核实伊拉克领土是否被用于袭击它们。扎伊迪的发言人努曼未回答有关调查状态的问题。

（路透社报道；尤塞夫·萨巴撰写；亚历山德拉·扎维斯编辑）
articles.summary:
LOADINGERROR LOADING
&

June 19 (Reuters) - Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) has set up secretive new cells in Iraq to carry out attacks on Gulf countries that host American forces, bypassing established militia networks to avoid detection, eight Iraqi sources told Reuters.

Three or four cells, each comprising about 10 elite Iraqi Shi’ite Muslim fighters, launched at least seven drone attacks from desert locations near the southern cities of Basra and Samawa against sites in Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates between April 20 and May 17, three of the sources said.

Advertisement

A number of their members were drawn from Islamic Resistance in Iraq, an umbrella group of hardline Shi’ite factions with thousands of fighters. But the new groups operate outside its command structure, reporting directly to the IRGC, according to the sources, who include two Iraqi military officials, another security official and five local militia commanders.

The establishment of the new Iraqi cells, which has not previously been reported, reflects a shift in IRGC tactics aimed at preserving Iran’s ability to project force across the region at a time when its armed proxy groups are greatly diminished and its own military and economic resources are depleted, the five militia commanders said.

Iraq, a Shi’ite-majority country, has a host of militias, many of which maintain close ties to Tehran. They form a key pillar of Iran’s regional “Axis of Resistance,” stretching from Gaza and Lebanon to Yemen and Iraq.

Advertisement

Groups acting under the banner of Islamic Resistance in Iraq have claimed responsibility for dozens of drone and rocket attacks against American assets in the country, drawing deadly retaliatory airstrikes, since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28. But there has been no mass mobilization of Iran’s proxies inside Iraq’s borders.

Several powerful Shi’ite factions there have been signaling since last year that they are ready to disarm and focus on domestic politics to avert an escalating conflict with the administration of U.S. President Donald Trump. That development may have spurred the IRGC to set up groups under its direct control, according to Jasim al-Bahadli, a retired Iraqi army general, and two lawmakers from the Shi’ite governing alliance.

Two of these factions, Asaib Ahl al-Haq and the Imam Ali Brigades, announced this month that they would begin surrendering their weapons to state authorities following repeated U.S. warnings to Iraq’s government to disband armed groups operating on its soil.

Advertisement

“The newer groups established by the IRGC appear smaller, more ideologically hardened and more tightly controlled, reflecting Iran’s need to conserve resources amid economic strain,” said Bahadli, who is an expert on Shi’ite armed groups.

U.S.-Iran Deal Does Not Address Tehran’s Support For Proxies

The U.S. and Iranian presidents signed an interim agreement on Wednesday to end the war, with negotiations to follow on difficult issues like the future of Tehran’s nuclear program. But Iranian officials have said Tehran’s support for “resistance groups” is not up for discussion, and the agreement does not address the issue.

Advertisement

Iran’s foreign ministry and its missions to the United Nations in New York and Geneva did not immediately respond to detailed questions for this article.

The U.S. State Department reiterated “expectations that the Iraqi government take immediate measures to dismantle all the tools of Iran’s destabilizing activities in Iraq to include the IRGC and Iran-aligned terrorist militias in Iraq.”

At a meeting on Monday, Iraq’s new prime minister, Ali al-Zaidi, and U.S. envoy Tom Barrack discussed Iraqi plans to ensure “the complete disarmament and disbandment of all armed groups” operating outside Iraqi state control and to ensure “Iraqi territory cannot be used by any side to threaten regional peace,” according to a joint statement.

Advertisement

Zaidi’s military spokesman, Sabah al-Numan, declined to comment for this article.

Kuwait’s information ministry, the Saudi government communications office and the UAE foreign ministry did not respond to requests for comment.

The war in Iran has battered the world’s most important energy-producing region, disrupting supplies and sending inflation surging. Tehran responded to U.S.-Israeli bombing runs by effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of the world’s trade in oil and liquefied natural gas passes, and launching a sweeping campaign of drone and missile strikes on Gulf neighbors.

Advertisement

New groups that emerged in Iraq during the conflict, often operating under unfamiliar names and with minimal public profiles, carried out at least three drone attacks targeting Kuwait, two targeting Saudi Arabia and two aimed at the UAE, the three Iraqi security sources said, citing a combination of human intelligence, intercepted communications and evidence gathered from launch sites.

Targets included Kuwait’s Ali Al Salem Air Base, where U.S. forces are deployed, and a military terminal at the country’s international airport, the sources said without elaborating. The attacks aimed at Saudi Arabia and the UAE were intercepted, according to the sources who could not confirm the intended targets.

Reuters could not independently verify their accounts.

An Early Test For Iraq’s New Prime Minister

Iraqi officials said the IRGC turned to the new cells to maintain plausible deniability, deflect blame from the country’s main Iran-backed groups and reduce U.S. pressure on Baghdad to disarm them.

Advertisement

The Iraqi security forces have limited information about the groups but are working to uncover their chains of command to help prevent future attacks, the officials said. The groups include elite fighters with expertise in drone operations and communications, they added.

Tehran spent decades and billions of dollars building up its network of regional alliances, which has been severely weakened since the Iran-backed Palestinian militant group Hamas attacked Israel on October 7, 2023.

Israel has hammered Hamas in Gaza and Hezbollah in Lebanon, while the Houthi movement in Yemen has been targeted by U.S. and British airstrikes. Syrian President Bashar al-Assad was toppled in December 2024, cutting off an important supply route for Iraqi militias and further isolating the Islamic Republic.

Advertisement

Rather than maintain a broad network of well-funded groups in Iraq, Iran now appears to be relying on a limited number of “more radicalized cadres willing to operate with leaner financial support, prioritizing loyalty, deniability and operational impact over mass recruitment,” said Bahadli, the militia expert.

The new groups pose an early test for Iraq’s Zaidi, who took office last month following U.S. pressure on the dominant alliance of Shi’ite political blocs to prevent the return of former Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki, who has close ties to Iran. Baghdad has long walked a tightrope between its two closest allies, Washington and Tehran, a balancing act that became more difficult during the war.

Attacks emanating from Iraq also risk unraveling Baghdad’s painstaking efforts to rebuild ties with wealthy Gulf neighbors, which have been strained since Saddam Hussein invaded Kuwait in 1990 but had started to thaw in recent years.

Advertisement

Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and the UAE summoned Iraq’s envoys in April to protest the strikes.

Iraqi authorities are investigating whether they include a May 17 drone attack that caused a fire at the Barakah Nuclear Power Plant, security officials said. Saudi Arabia said it intercepted three drones that entered its airspace from Iraq the same day, an attack the Iraqi officials said was carried out by a new group.

Zaidi condemned the two attacks, describing them as criminal acts, and promised a joint inquiry with both Gulf countries to verify whether Iraqi territory was used to target them. Numan, Zaidi’s spokesman, did not answer questions about the status of the investigation.

(Reporting by Reuters; Writing by Yousef Saba; Editing by Alexandra Zavis)
